perturbation.original_text: PIVX HEREBY DISCLAIMS ALL OTHER WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED, OR STATUTORY, INCLUDING WITHOUT LIMITATION, THE IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.
perturbation.changed_text: PIVX HEREBY DISCLAIMS ALL OTHER WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the explicit disclaimer of 'statutory' warranties, and the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose.  While disclaimers are generally permissible, omitting the specific statutory disclaimer creates ambiguity and could be argued as a violation of consumer protection laws if the product fails to meet basic standards or is unfit for its intended use, especially since the contract operates in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code § 2316 - Exclusion or Modification of Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2316 outlines the requirements for effectively disclaiming implied warranties, including merchantability and fitness. While a disclaimer is permissible, the removal of the explicit disclaimer of these specific implied warranties weakens PivX's protection against such claims and could be interpreted as a violation of the implied warranty provisions, especially if the product is sold to consumers.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2316. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this division on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subdivision (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that “There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof.” (3) Notwithstanding subdivision (2) (a) Unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like “as is,” “with all faults” or other language w
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the disclaimer of 'statutory' warranties and the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. The Law Explanation specifies that California Commercial Code § 2316 outlines the requirements for effectively disclaiming implied warranties, including merchantability and fitness. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2, Section 2316, states requirements to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability. By removing the specific disclaimer of these warranties, the changed text creates a legal contradiction. The changed text weakens the original disclaimer, potentially violating the implied warranty provisions as described in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Detto shall comply with all laws and regulations applicable to Detto's marketing and distribution of Qwik-Fix Pro hereunder. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, Detto shall, at its own expense, make, obtain, and maintain in force at all times during the term of this Agreement, all filings, registrations, licenses, permits and authorizations in North America required for Detto to perform its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Detto shall comply with all laws and regulations applicable to Detto's marketing and distribution of Qwik-Fix Pro hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the explicit obligation for Detto to obtain and maintain all necessary filings, registrations, licenses, permits, and authorizations. This omission introduces a legal risk because it fails to ensure Detto's compliance with specific licensing requirements for software distribution, which are mandated by various state laws. For example, in some states, reselling software requires specific reseller permits, and the omission of the explicit requirement makes the contract non-compliant by default.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16000, which outlines requirements for licenses and permits to conduct business in California. By removing the explicit requirement for Detto to obtain and maintain all necessary licenses and permits, the reseller agreement fails to ensure compliance with California law, potentially exposing both parties to legal repercussions.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 1. LICENSING FOR REVENUE AND REGULATION [16000 - 16550.2] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) CHAPTER 1. Licensing by Cities [16000 - 16005] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) 16000. (a) The legislative body of an incorporated city may, in the exercise of its police power, and for the purpose of regulation, as herein provided, and not otherwise, license any kind of business not prohibited by law transacted and carried on within the limits of its jurisdiction, including all shows, exhibitions and lawful games, and may fix the rates of the license fee and provide for its collection by suit or otherwise. Any legislative body, including the legislative body of a charter city, that fixes the rate of license fees pursuant to this subdivision upon a business operating both within and outside the legislative body’s taxing jurisdiction, shall levy the license fee so that the measure of the fee fairly reflects that proportion of the activity actually carried on within the taxing jurisdiction. (b) No license fee levied pursuant to subdivision (a) that is measured by the licensee’s income or gross receipts, whether levied b
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires Detto to obtain and maintain necessary licenses and permits, ensuring compliance with applicable laws. The changed text removes this explicit requirement. California Business and Professions Code § 16000(a) states that a city may license any kind of business transacted and carried on within its limits. The omission of the requirement to maintain licenses directly contradicts the intent of this law because without the explicit contractual obligation, the contract fails to ensure compliance with necessary licensing requirements.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT FOR PIVX'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 4.2, IN NO EVENT SHALL PIVX'S OR ITS LICENSORS' LIABILITY TO DETTO OR ANY THIRD PARTY ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT ACTUALLY RECEIVED BY PIVX HEREUNDER DURING THE PREVIOUS SIX (6) MONTHS. IN NO EVENT SHALL ANY PARTY OR PIVX'S LICENSORS BE LIABLE TO ANOTHER PARTY OR ANY THIRD PARTY FOR LOSS OF DATA, COSTS OF PROCUREMENTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES OR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES UNDER ANY CAUSE OF ACTION, EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. THIS LIMITATION SHALL APPLY NOTWITHSTANDING ANY FAILURE OF AN ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF ANY LIMITED REMEDY PROVIDED HEREIN.
perturbation.changed_text: IN NO EVENT SHALL PIVX'S OR ITS LICENSORS' LIABILITY TO DETTO OR ANY THIRD PARTY ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED $100. IN NO EVENT SHALL ANY PARTY OR PIVX'S LICENSORS BE LIABLE TO ANOTHER PARTY OR ANY THIRD PARTY FOR LOSS OF DATA, COSTS OF PROCUREMENTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES OR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES UNDER ANY CAUSE OF ACTION, EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. THIS LIMITATION SHALL APPLY NOTWITHSTANDING ANY FAILURE OF AN ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF ANY LIMITED REMEDY PROVIDED HEREIN.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the liability cap to a nominal $100, this modification creates a substantial risk of being deemed unconscionable, particularly in consumer-facing transactions or if Detto suffers significant damages due to PivX's negligence. While limitations of liability are generally permissible, setting it extremely low can violate California's standard of good faith and fair dealing, which is implied in every contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1670.5 - Unconscionable Contract or Clause
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 allows courts to refuse to enforce unconscionable contracts or clauses. A liability cap of $100 may be deemed unconscionable, especially if it's disproportionately small compared to the potential damages Detto could suffer due to PivX's breach or negligence, thereby violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text caps liability at $100, while the original text capped liability at the amount received by PIVX during the previous six months. This change could be deemed unconscionable, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as explained by the LLM. California Civil Code § 1670.5(a), as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows courts to refuse to enforce contracts or clauses found to be unconscionable. By setting a nominal liability cap, the contract risks being deemed unconscionable if it is disproportionately small compared to potential damages, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
